title: How do you find the best time to buy and sell a stock?
description: Maximize profit from a single buy/sell.
answer: Track the **minimum price** seen so far and, at each day, compute `price - minSoFar` to update the best profit. One pass updates both, giving O(n) time and O(1) space. The trick is realizing you only need the lowest price to the left of each selling day.

title: How do you compute the product of an array except self without division?
answer: Build a **prefix product** (product of everything to the left) and a **suffix product** (everything to the right); the answer for index i is `prefix[i] * suffix[i]`. Do it in two passes, reusing the output array, for O(n) time and O(1) extra space. Division is disallowed because of zeros.

title: How do you solve the container with most water problem?
answer: Place pointers at both ends; the area is `min(height[l], height[r]) * (r - l)`. Always move the **shorter** wall inward, since keeping it can never increase the area. This greedy two-pointer sweep is O(n) versus the O(n²) brute force.

title: How do you solve the trapping rain water problem?
answer: Water above each bar = `min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]`. Use **two pointers** moving inward, tracking the running left/right maxima and adding trapped water on the smaller side. O(n) time, O(1) space — no need to precompute full max arrays.

title: How do you group anagrams together?
answer: Use a **hash map** keyed by a canonical form of each word — either the sorted characters (O(n·k log k)) or a 26-length count signature (O(n·k)). Words sharing a key go in the same bucket. The signature key avoids the sort cost.

title: How do you find the longest substring without repeating characters?
answer: Expand a **sliding window** with a set/map of last-seen indices. When you hit a repeat, jump the left edge past the previous occurrence. Track the max window length throughout — O(n) time, O(min(n, charset)) space.

title: How do you find the minimum window substring?
description: Explain minimum window covering all chars.
answer: Use a **variable sliding window** with a need-count map of the target characters. Expand right until the window covers all needs, then shrink left while still valid, recording the smallest. Each character enters and leaves once — O(n + m).

title: How do you solve the 3Sum problem?
description: Explain finding triplets summing to zero.
answer: **Sort** the array, then fix one element and use **two pointers** on the rest to find pairs summing to its negation. Skip duplicates at each level to avoid repeated triplets. Sorting is O(n log n) and the scan is O(n²) overall.

title: How do you search in a rotated sorted array?
answer: Run **binary search**, but at each step decide which half is sorted by comparing `nums[mid]` to the ends. If the target lies within the sorted half's range, search there; otherwise search the other half. Still O(log n) despite the rotation.

title: How do you find a peak element in an array?
description: Explain peak finding in O(log n).
answer: A peak is greater than its neighbors. Use **binary search**: if `nums[mid] < nums[mid+1]`, a peak must lie to the right, so go right; otherwise go left (including mid). This converges to a peak in O(log n) without scanning everything.

title: How do you count subarrays that sum to k?
answer: Maintain a running **prefix sum** and a hash map of how many times each prefix sum has occurred. For each index, the number of subarrays ending there with sum k equals the count of `prefixSum - k` seen so far. O(n) time and space.

title: How do you traverse a matrix in spiral order?
answer: Maintain four boundaries — top, bottom, left, right — and walk right, down, left, up, shrinking the relevant boundary after each pass. Stop when boundaries cross. It visits each cell once in O(rows × cols).

title: How do you rotate an N×N matrix by 90 degrees in place?
answer: **Transpose** the matrix (swap `m[i][j]` with `m[j][i]`), then **reverse each row**. That combination gives a clockwise 90° rotation in place with O(1) extra space and O(n²) time. Reversing columns instead rotates counter-clockwise.

title: How do you find the longest palindromic substring?
answer: **Expand around each center** — every index and every gap between indices — growing outward while characters match, and keep the longest. There are 2n−1 centers, each expansion O(n), so O(n²) time, O(1) space. Manacher's algorithm does it in O(n).

title: How do you find the single number where every other element appears twice?
answer: **XOR** all elements together. Pairs cancel (`x ^ x = 0`) and `x ^ 0 = x`, so the lone element remains. O(n) time, O(1) space — no hash set needed. Variants with triples need bit-count tricks.

title: How do you find the majority element?
answer: **Boyer-Moore voting**: keep a candidate and a count; increment when you see the candidate, decrement otherwise, and switch candidate when count hits zero. The element appearing more than n/2 times survives. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: How do you find the missing number in 0..n?
answer: Sum 0..n with `n(n+1)/2` and subtract the actual array sum — the difference is the missing number, O(n) time / O(1) space. **XOR** of indices and values also works and avoids overflow risk.

title: How do you sort an array of 0s, 1s, and 2s?
description: Explain the Dutch National Flag algorithm.
answer: **Dutch National Flag**: keep `low`, `mid`, `high` pointers. Sweep `mid`: swap 0s to the front (low), leave 1s, swap 2s to the back (high). One pass partitions all three values in O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: How do you add one to a number represented as a digit array?
answer: Walk from the **last digit**: if it's < 9, increment and return; if it's 9, set it to 0 and carry to the left. If you carry past the front (all 9s), prepend a 1. O(n) in the worst case, O(1) usually.

title: How do you merge two sorted linked lists?
answer: Use a **dummy head** and a tail pointer; repeatedly attach the smaller of the two list heads and advance it. When one list runs out, append the rest of the other. O(m+n) time, O(1) extra space. The dummy node avoids special-casing the head.

title: How do you remove the nth node from the end of a linked list?
answer: Advance a **fast** pointer n nodes ahead, then move fast and **slow** together until fast reaches the end — slow now sits just before the target. Use a dummy head so removing the first node is uniform. One pass, O(n).

title: How do you add two numbers stored as linked lists?
answer: Walk both lists together, summing corresponding digits plus a **carry**, creating a new node for `sum % 10` and carrying `sum / 10`. Continue until both lists and the carry are exhausted. A dummy head simplifies building the result. O(max(m,n)).

title: How do you find the intersection node of two linked lists?
answer: Advance two pointers, one per list; when a pointer hits the end, redirect it to the **other list's head**. After at most one switch each, they align and meet at the intersection (or both reach null). O(m+n) time, O(1) space.

title: How do you find the node where a linked list cycle begins?
answer: First detect the cycle with **Floyd's** slow/fast pointers. Then reset one pointer to the head and advance both one step at a time — they meet at the **cycle's start**. This works because of the equal-distance property from the meeting point. O(n), O(1).

title: How do you check if a linked list is a palindrome?
answer: Find the **middle** with slow/fast pointers, **reverse the second half**, then compare it node-by-node with the first half. Optionally restore the list afterward. O(n) time and O(1) space, beating the O(n)-space copy-to-array approach.
